title: Alcaraz Out of French Open: Defending Champion Sidelined by Wrist Injury
summary:
Two-time defending champion Carlos Alcaraz will miss the French Open next month due to an ongoing wrist injury, he announced on Instagram. The 22-year-old world number two sustained the injury to his right wrist during his first-round victory at the Barcelona Open last week. Subsequent tests led him to withdraw from both the Italian Open and the French Open, which are played on clay courts.

"After the results of the tests carried out today, we have decided that the most prudent thing to do is to be cautious and not participate in Rome or Roland Garros, while we assess the situation to determine when we can return to the court," Alcaraz posted. "This is a difficult time for me, but I am sure we will come out of this stronger."

Alcaraz began 2026 by winning his first Australian Open title, becoming the youngest man to complete a career Grand Slam—winning all four major tournaments at least once. He has won seven Grand Slam titles overall, including five of the last nine. His long-term rival, Jannik Sinner, overtook him for the world number one ranking this month after defeating him in the Monte Carlo final. Sinner now has a chance to secure his own career Grand Slam, as the French Open is the only major missing from his collection.

Speaking after a match at the Madrid Open, Sinner expressed sympathy: "It is very sad news. Being that young like he is and like I am, we need to look at our bodies first before worse things [happen]. Hopefully he can be back for Wimbledon and we all hope for great battles in the future."

Since qualifying for his first Grand Slam in 2021, Alcaraz has only missed one major—the 2023 Australian Open due to a hamstring injury. His absence from Roland Garros opens the draw significantly, as he was considered the best player on clay by many, including Sinner.
why_it_matters: Alcaraz's absence shows the physical toll elite sports take on even the best young athletes, raising questions about workload, injury management, and whether the tennis calendar pushes players past safe limits — issues that affect junior athletes and the future of competitive sports.

keywords.explanation: A 22-year-old Spanish tennis player who is world number two and defending French Open champion.
Winning all four major tennis tournaments (Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, US Open) at least once.
Alcaraz's rival and current world number one tennis player from Italy.
The stadium in Paris that hosts the French Open tennis tournament.
To pull out of a competition because of injury or other reasons.
An injury to the large muscle at the back of the thigh, which Alcaraz had before in 2023.

questions.question: Why will Carlos Alcaraz miss the French Open?
Which tournament did Alcaraz win in 2026 before his injury?
What is a 'career Grand Slam'?
options: Winning three majors in a row
Winning all four major tournaments at least once
Winning the same major five times
Being ranked number one for a year
questions.correct_answer: Winning all four major tournaments at least once

questions.question: Who took over the world number one ranking from Alcaraz?
How did Jannik Sinner react to Alcaraz's withdrawal?
options: He was happy to have less competition
He expressed sympathy and said health comes first
He said Alcaraz should have played
questions.correct_answer: He expressed sympathy and said health comes first

questions.question: Which major tournament did Alcaraz miss in 2023 due to injury?

background_read: Carlos Alcaraz (a 22-year-old Spanish tennis prodigy) became world number one after winning the US Open in 2022. He is known for his explosive power and versatility on all surfaces. Completing a career Grand Slam at his age is a rare achievement, matched only by legends like Roger Federer, Rafael Nadal, and Novak Djokovic.

The French Open (also called Roland Garros, after the stadium in Paris) is the only Grand Slam played on clay, a surface that rewards stamina and sliding. Alcaraz had been considered the top clay-court player by many, including his rival Jannik Sinner (the Italian who now holds the number one ranking).

Tennis injuries often disrupt young careers; Alcaraz previously missed the 2023 Australian Open with a hamstring injury. Sinner, who now has a clear path at the French Open, is aiming to win his first career French Open, which would complete his own career Grand Slam.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Alcaraz announces withdrawal from French Open due to wrist injury.
└─ DETAIL: Injury sustained during Barcelona Open first-round win.
└─ TESTS: Led to withdrawal from Italian Open and French Open.
QUOTE: Alcaraz's Instagram statement: 'prudent... cautious... assess situation'.
ACHIEVEMENTS: Won Australian Open 2026, became youngest to complete career Grand Slam.
└─ STATS: 7 Grand Slam titles, 5 of last 9 majors won.
CONTEXT: Rival Jannik Sinner overtook Alcaraz for world number one after Monte Carlo final.
└─ SINNER'S REACTION: 'very sad news... look at our bodies first'.
INJURY HISTORY: Only missed one previous major (2023 Australian Open due to hamstring).
IMPLICATIONS: 'opens the draw significantly' — Sinner now favorite on clay.

perspectives.description: Alcaraz's choice to be 'cautious' prioritizes long-term health over short-term competition, setting a responsible example for young athletes who may feel pressure to play through pain.

The defending champion's withdrawal disappoints fans and weakens the French Open draw. It also puts pressure on the tournament organizers to address player burnout and scheduling that may lead to injuries.

Injuries are a recurring theme in tennis; Alcaraz's missed 2023 Australian Open and this withdrawal suggest a pattern of physical vulnerability even among top players, possibly linked to the demanding tour schedule.

If Alcaraz returns for Wimbledon as hoped, the rivalry with Sinner could intensify. The door is also open for new contenders at Roland Garros, which may reshape the men's tennis hierarchy in the coming months.
